topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. I stared at the screen, the bright white text blurring into meaningless shapes. *Party cancelled.* My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake that did little to fill my lungs. A strange, cold knot tightened in my chest, radiating outward. I reread the message three times, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that might somehow negate the reality sinking in. The silence of my apartment suddenly felt enormous, amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. I wanted to stand up, to pace, to do anything but sit here, paralyzed by the sudden deflation of expectation. My hands started to tremble, and I pressed them against my eyes, trying to scrub the words away, but the image of the planned celebration—the laughter, the decorations, the shared joy—flashed behind my eyelids, sharp and painful.

[story 2]

When I finally saw the notification, the color seemed to drain from the room. It wasn't anger that surfaced first, but a deep, hollow ache, like something vital had been abruptly withdrawn. I let the phone slip from my fingers, the sound barely audible against the quiet hum of the refrigerator. My shoulders slumped instantly, a heavy, defeated weight settling across my back. I walked to the window, looking out at the street, but nothing registered. The world outside seemed muted, the vibrant colors of the afternoon washed out. A slow, hot prickle began behind my eyes, and I instinctively raised a hand to rub my forehead, a gesture of deep discomfort. Every muscle in my jaw felt clenched, holding back a wave of profound disappointment that threatened to spill over into a shaky, uncontrolled sigh.

[story 3]

"Cancelled." The single word echoed in the empty space where excitement had been moments before. I felt a distinct tightness in my throat, a strange constriction that made swallowing feel like sandpaper. My gaze drifted down to my hands, noticing the slight tremor that was beginning in my fingers. It wasn't sadness, exactly; it was more like a sudden imbalance, a disconcerting wobble in my equilibrium. I stood motionless for a long time, listening to the silence of the apartment, which now felt accusatory. A watery sheen started to gather in the corners of my eyes, and I blinked hard, trying to clear the moisture, but it only seemed to confirm the deflation in my spirit. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to regulate the sudden, uneven rhythm of my own heart.
